sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Software engineering combines a defined development process, technical methods for analysis/design/testing, and supporting tools for automation and control. These three layers work together to produce and maintain software systematically, so all are integrated.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Process alone gives management structure, methods alone give techniques, and tools alone automate tasks; none by itself constitutes the full discipline.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Software engineering is commonly described as a layered technology of process, methods, and tools.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: If investigation finds no cause inside the software system, the failure is attributed to an event or condition outside the program's controlled boundary—an external error. Examples include unavailable devices, malformed external services, or environmental failures.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Internal and logic errors originate in the program's code/state. An inherent error is rooted in the problem statement or requirements, which is still an identifiable source rather than an unidentified internal cause.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Classify an error by whether its cause lies inside or outside the software boundary.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Measured software attributes—size, complexity, defect density, effort, change rate, and similar metrics—support cost/schedule estimation (i), reliability prediction (ii), and monitoring/control of development (iii). Therefore i, ii, and iii are valid uses.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Statement iv ('nowhere') contradicts all three established uses, so every option containing iv is false.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Software metrics support prediction as well as ongoing project management.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Black-box testing treats the implementation as hidden and checks observable behavior at its interfaces: supplied inputs, produced outputs, and conformance to requirements. Option A states this purpose.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Examining internal code structure is white-box testing. Black-box testing is not defined by being conducted only at a client's site.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Black box tests external behavior without using internal implementation knowledge.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: The stem describes a particular batch/transaction-processing naming convention: after the operator supplies the source transaction filename and editing prepares the transactions for execution, the resulting executable transaction file is named Opt.Exe. Thus the requested stipulated filename is option C.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: Batch.Exe, Trans.Exe, and Edit.Exe are plausible descriptive names but are not the output-file name specified by this convention.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: This is a terminology/naming question about the edited transaction-processing output, not a general operating-system rule.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: A single-stage interconnection network may need to send an item through the same switching stage several times before it reaches its destination. Because data can circulate through that stage repeatedly, it is called a recirculating network.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: One-sided/two-sided describe neither this routing behavior nor the standard name, and a pipeline normally uses a sequence of distinct stages.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: Repeated passes through one switching stage give the name recirculating network.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: Online Analytical Processing (OLAP) supports multidimensional, aggregate, and exploratory queries over large databases/data warehouses to retrieve decision-support information.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: OLTP is optimized for numerous short operational transactions and updates. OLDP and OLPP are not the standard term for large-scale analytical querying.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: OLTP runs operations; OLAP analyzes accumulated data.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: MMDS (Multichannel Multipoint Distribution Service) distributes multiple channels from a fixed transmitter to subscriber antennas using microwave radio. Because it can provide cable-like multichannel service without physical coaxial cable, it has historically been called 'wireless cable.'
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: ATM is a cell-switching technology and CDMA a multiple-access technique. LMDS is also a fixed wireless access technology, but the historical nickname in this question is conventionally associated with MMDS.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: MMDS is a point-to-multipoint microwave distribution system historically marketed as wireless cable.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: IEEE 802.11a is one member of the IEEE 802.11 wireless LAN family marketed under the Wi-Fi interoperability name. It operates in the 5 GHz range and is therefore Wi-Fi.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: WiMAX is based on IEEE 802.16, Fast Ethernet is IEEE 802.3 networking, and 802.11g is a different Wi-Fi amendment rather than another name for 802.11a.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: 802.11 denotes Wi-Fi WLAN standards; 802.16 denotes WiMAX.

sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.explanation: The Unlicensed National Information Infrastructure (U-NII) allocations are in the 5 GHz region (split into several sub-bands). Therefore the appropriate listed frequency is 5 GHz.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.whyOthersFail: 2.4 GHz is associated with the ISM band used by 802.11b/g and other devices; 33 MHz and 5 MHz are far outside the U-NII microwave allocations.
sources/ugc-net-cs-2009-dec-paper-2.pdf#.keyIdea: U-NII is the unlicensed 5-GHz-band family used by technologies including 802.11a.
